retrieved_docs.title: Lissencephaly -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: LIS1 (PAFAH1B1) is the most studied. This gene is located on chromosome 17p13.3. [8] LIS1 regulates the motor protein dynein, which is linked to neuronal nuclei movement along microtubules. [9] The mutation or deletion in the LIS1 gene is associated with both isolated Lissencephaly syndrome and Miller-Dieker syndrome. [8] DCX

retrieved_docs.title: Lissencephaly -- Differential Diagnosis
retrieved_docs.content: Examination of the brain in type I lissencephaly shows a cerebral cortex with four layers instead of six layers as in normal patients, while in type 2 lissencephaly the cerebral cortex is disorganized, and appears pebbled or nodular due to complete displacement of the cerebral cortex with clusters of cortical neurons separated by glio-mesenchymal tissue. The patients also had abnormalities of muscle and eyes. [20] Classic lissencephaly (type 1): LIS1: Isolated lissencephaly and Miller-Dieker syndrome (lissencephaly associated with facial dysmorphism). [30] LISX1: DCX gene mutation. Compared to lissencephaly caused by LIS1 mutations, DCX shows a six-layered cortex instead of four. Isolated lissencephaly without other known genetic defects Cobblestone lissencephaly (type 2): Walker-Warburg syndrome Fukuyama syndrome Muscle-eye-brain disease Other types cannot be placed in one of the two aforementioned groups:

retrieved_docs.title: Lissencephaly -- Differential Diagnosis
retrieved_docs.content: LIS2: Norman-Roberts syndrome, similar to type I lissencephaly or Miller-Dieker syndrome, but without deletion of the chromosome 17. LIS3 LISX2 Microlissencephaly: It is a combination of the absence of normal cerebral cortex folding and an abnormally small head. Children with usual lissencephaly have a normal head size at birth. In children with reduced head size at birth, microlissencephaly is typically diagnosed. It is also important to differentiate between lissencephaly and polymicrogyria, which is a different developmental malformation of the brain.

retrieved_docs.title: Cat Eye Syndrome (Schmid-Fraccaro Syndrome) -- Etiology -- Genotype-Phenotype Correlations
retrieved_docs.content: Although no strict correlation exists between marker size and clinical severity, larger markers that include additional 22q11 material tend to cause more pronounced malformations. Mosaicism typically results in a milder phenotype. The presence of 2 intact centromeres may increase the mitotic stability of the marker chromosome, often leading to uniform tetrasomy across multiple tissues. [17]

retrieved_docs.title: Genetics, Nondisjunction -- Clinical Significance -- Autosomal Trisomies
retrieved_docs.content: Patau syndrome: Trisomy of chromosome 13 Clinical Features: Rocker-bottom feet, microphthalmia (abnormally small eyes), microcephaly (abnormally small head), polydactyly, holoprosencephaly, cleft lip and palate, congenital heart disease, and severe intellectual disability. Life expectancy is seldom longer than one year. Edwards syndrome: Trisomy of chromosome 18 Clinical Features: Rocker-bottom feet, low set ears, micrognathia (abnormally small jaw), clenched hands with overlapping fingers, congenital heart disease, and severe intellectual disability. Life expectancy is normally less than one year. Down syndrome: Trisomy of chromosome 21 The most common viable aneuploidy. Clinical Features: Single palmar crease, flat facies, prominent epicanthal folds, duodenal atresia, congenital heart disease, Hirschsprung disease, intellectual disability. Notably increased risk to develop Alzheimer's disease or leukemia. Life expectancy is about 60 years.

retrieved_docs.title: Dandy-Walker Malformation -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: The majority of cases are sporadic. However, some may result from chromosomal aneuploidy, Mendelian disorders, and environmental exposures, including congenital rubella and fetal alcohol exposure. This condition underscores the intricate interplay between genetic and environmental factors in brain development. [9] Several conditions have been reported to be associated with DWM: Mendelian disorders: Warburg syndrome Coffin-Siris syndrome Fraser cryptophthalmos Joubert–Boltshauser syndrome Meckel–Gruber syndrome Aicardi syndrome Neurofibromatosis type 1 [14] Chromosomal aberrations: Duplication syndromes, including those involving 5q; 8p; 8q; 17q Trisomies 9, 13, and 18 Triploidy Deletion at 6(p24–p25) 9ph+ heteromorphism Environmental agents: Prenatal exposure to rubella, cytomegalovirus, toxoplasmosis, coumadin, alcohol Maternal diabetes Sporadic syndromes: Klippel–Feil syndrome Goldenhar syndrome Cornelia de Lange syndrome

retrieved_docs.title: Hurler Syndrome -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: Hurler syndrome is an autosomal recessive disorder due to a defective gene which encodes for the enzyme alpha-L-iduronidase (IUDA) that is located on chromosome 4. [1]

retrieved_docs.title: Chromosome Instability Syndromes -- Pathophysiology -- Nijmegen Breakage Syndrome
retrieved_docs.content: Nijmegen breakage syndrome is an autosomal recessive chromosome instability syndrome associated with immunodeficiency. Nijmegen breakage syndrome results from mutations in the nibrin ( NBN ) gene on 8q21. The protein product is involved in DNA double-strand breaks repair, base excision repair, meiotic recombination, and telomere maintenance. [21] [22]

retrieved_docs.title: Cat Eye Syndrome (Schmid-Fraccaro Syndrome) -- Evaluation -- Genetic and cytogenetic testing
retrieved_docs.content: Conventional karyotyping: This typically reveals a small supernumerary marker chromosome derived from chromosome 22 (inv dup(22)(q11)). Fluorescence in situ hybridization: FISH using a targeted probe for the 22q11 region can confirm the duplication. Array comparative genomic hybridization or single-nucleotide polymorphism microarray: This is used to precisely map the breakpoints and the extent of copy-number gain within the 22q11.1-q11.21 region, aiding in genotype-phenotype correlation. Parental studies: These studies help determine whether the marker chromosome is de novo or inherited, which is essential for recurrence risk counseling. [32]

retrieved_docs.title: Cat Eye Syndrome (Schmid-Fraccaro Syndrome) -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: CES arises from a chromosomal imbalance involving the proximal region of chromosome 22 (22q11.1-q11.2). In over 90% of affected individuals, this manifests as a supernumerary marker chromosome—often dicentric—harboring 2 additional copies of an approximately 1.5 to 2 Mb critical region, resulting in partial tetrasomy of 22q11.1-q11.2. A minority of cases exhibit a similarly derived marker with only an additional copy, leading to partial trisomy. This marker chromosome typically includes dosage-sensitive genes whose overexpression disrupts normal embryonic development across multiple organ systems. [10]

retrieved_docs.title: Jacobsen Syndrome (11q Terminal Deletion Syndrome) -- Prognosis
retrieved_docs.content: As there is no curative therapy for Jacobsen syndrome, prognosis is often determined by the severity of cardiac or hematologic disease. There is a 20% risk of mortality within the first 2 years of life, often due to complications of cardiac defects or after high-risk cardiothoracic surgery. The overall life expectancy is largely unknown, although the oldest documented patients have reached 45 years of age. [2] Patients with Jacobsen syndrome may require surgical and medical interventions throughout their lifetime, reflecting the ongoing need for specialized care and management.

retrieved_docs.title: Jacobsen Syndrome (11q Terminal Deletion Syndrome) -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: Jacobsen syndrome is diagnosed through a combination of clinical examination, imaging studies, and genetic testing. Approximately 50% of patients are diagnosed within the first year of life, whereas those with milder presentations may not receive a diagnosis until later.

retrieved_docs.content: The qualifier “vrozené,” which denotes a congenital origin, can be applied to several disorders, indicating that they may be present from birth. It is an allowed qualifier for ناسور مَثانِيٌّ مَهْبِلِيّ, قَرْحَة مَعِدِيّة, انسداد صفراوي خارج الكبد, فرط البروتينات الشحمية, and الْتِهابُ المثانة. Recognizing these conditions as congenital guides clinicians toward appropriate genetic evaluation, early diagnostic testing, and tailored management plans that address their lifelong nature.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 2 -- Community 930
retrieved_docs.content: Cerebral arteriopathy, autosomal dominant, with subcortical infarcts and leukoencephalopathy, type 1 is a hereditary cerebrovascular disorder that characteristically presents with a gait disorder (اضطراب المِشْيَة). The disease’s underlying vascular pathology leads to subcortical infarctions and diffuse white‑matter changes, which together disrupt motor pathways and result in abnormal walking patterns. Recognizing gait disturbance as a key manifestation of this specific type of cerebral arteriopathy aids clinicians in diagnosing the condition and guiding appropriate genetic counseling and management strategies.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 2 -- Community 633
retrieved_docs.content: Diabetes (مرض السكري) is presented as a central disease that is linked to a wide spectrum of clinical conditions and genetic factors. Various forms of neonatal diabetes, including DIABETES MELLITUS, TRANSIENT NEONATAL, 1 and 2, as well as the syndrome described as Developmental Delay, Epilepsy, and Neonatal Diabetes, are all mapped to diabetes, indicating that early‑onset glucose dysregulation can coexist with neurodevelopmental abnormalities. Dysfunction of the human INSR protein is identified as a gene‑product malfunction that is associated with the development of diabetes, underscoring a molecular basis for the disorder. Clinically, diabetes is associated with several serious complications affecting multiple organ systems. Retinal disorder (اضطراب في الشبكية) and congestive heart failure (فشل قلبي احتقاني) are both noted as conditions that commonly co‑occur with diabetes, reflecting its impact on vascular and cardiac health. Metabolic syndrome (متلازمة الأيض) is listed as a child

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 2 -- Community 683
retrieved_docs.content: Diabetes (مرض السكري) is presented as a central disease that is linked to a wide range of clinical findings, genetic factors, therapeutic considerations, and related disorders. Various forms of neonatal diabetes, including Diabetes Mellitus, Transient Neonatal, 1, Diabetes Mellitus, Transient Neonatal, 2 (disorder), and the syndrome of Developmental Delay, Epilepsy, and Neonatal Diabetes, are all mapped to diabetes, highlighting its relevance from birth onward. Genetic dysfunction of the human INSR protein is specifically associated with diabetes, underscoring a molecular basis for the disease. Clinically, diabetes is associated with several severe complications of the lower extremities. Amputation of lower extremity includes child procedures such as toe amputation (بتر أصبع القدم) and below‑knee amputation (بتر تحت الركبة), both of which are clinically associated with diabetes. Necrosis of the amputation stump of the right lower limb is an associated morphology (النَخَر) and is linked

retrieved_docs.content: DIABETES MELLITUS, TRANSIENT NEONATAL, 2 (disorder), Diabetes Mellitus, Transient Neonatal, 1, and the syndrome described as Developmental Delay, Epilepsy, and Neonatal Diabetes are all mapped to the broader condition مرض السكري (diabetes mellitus). These specific neonatal and developmental presentations are therefore recognized as manifestations or subtypes of diabetes mellitus. In addition, مرض السكري is clinically associated with اضطراب في الشبكية, indicating that retinal disorders such as diabetic retinopathy commonly occur in patients with diabetes mellitus. Recognizing these relationships helps clinicians identify atypical early‑onset forms of diabetes and monitor for ocular complications.
